blocks.content: In the room on the other side of the wall, Marcus slept.

His water bottle was on the nightstand. His phone was charging on the floor. He'd left the balcony door cracked two inches because he ran warm and the night air was exactly right. On the desk under the window, beside his keys and his sunscreen and the novel he'd brought and probably wouldn't open, was a bag he hadn't fully unpacked — an old toiletry bag, dark blue canvas, that had been in rotation since college.

The bag had moved with him through four apartments, two cities, one storage unit, and every trip he'd taken for the better part of a decade. It went where he went because he'd never been the kind of person to fully unpack between trips — he'd just leave it stocked and grab it again the next time.

He hadn't opened the front zip pocket in years. He never used it. He kept meaning to clean out the old receipts and parking stubs and whatever else had accumulated there since the last time he'd actually gone through it.
He'd kept meaning to for so long it had stopped occurring to him.
The ocean came through the cracked balcony door, cool and even, the sound of something that has been doing what it does for longer than any of this.
In the room on the other side of the wall, Nadia finally slept too.
Between them, in the dark, in the front zip pocket of a dark blue canvas bag that nobody had opened in years:
Waiting, as it had waited for eight years, to become the most important thing in the room.
